Use plain `data` for a single strip, optional `group` per point for color, or `strips` for multiple horizontal rows (one beeswarm per category). Set `"accurate":false` to use force layout instead of exact x positions. Spec: { "type":"beeswarm", "title":"...", "xLabel":"...", "xScale":[0,100], "data":[{"label":"Germany","value":12},{"label":"France","value":23,"group":"Europe"}], "strips":[{"label":"2023","data":[{"label":"A","value":12}]}] }

box.promptDescription: - **box** — box-and-whisker plot; shows median, IQR, and outliers per category. Best for comparing value distributions across groups. Spec: { "type":"box", "title":"...", "yLabel":"...", "data":[{"label":"Category A","values":[10,20,30,25,15]}] }

chord.promptDescription: - **chord** — circular diagram showing directed flows or relationships between a set of categories arranged around a ring, where each arc is a category and each ribbon is the flow between two categories (ribbon thickness at each end encodes the value in that direction). Ideal for migration, navigation paths, message/traffic volume, or any square 'who-goes-to-whom' matrix. Provide `labels` (n names) and a square n×n `matrix` where matrix[i][j] is the flow FROM i TO j. Spec: { "type":"chord", "title":"...", "labels":["A","B","C"], "matrix":[[0,10,5],[10,0,8],[5,8,0]] }

crossset.promptDescription: - **crossset** — hierarchical bivariate matrix (CrossSet, IEEE VIS 2025) for jointly exploring two categorical/grouped dimensions at multiple levels of detail. Rows = first-dimension items grouped by a higher-level category; columns = second-dimension items grouped by a higher-level category. The overview shows group×group roll-up totals; clicking any group header expands it to individual items. Best for: product subcategory × territory revenue matrices, customer segment × product category analysis, employee × department cross-tabulations, or any two groupable categorical axes with a numeric measure. IMPORTANT: Use lazy SQL — always embed the SQL as `"sql":"..."` in the spec. Required SQL column aliases: `row_group` (row grouping key, e.g. ProductCategory), `row_label` (individual row item, e.g. ProductSubcategoryName or ProductName), `col_group` (column grouping key, e.g. CountryRegionName or SalesRegion), `col_label` (individual column item, e.g. TerritoryName), `value` (numeric measure, e.g. SUM(LineTotal) or COUNT(*)). Use GROUP BY row_group, row_label, col_group, col_label. Spec: { "type":"crossset", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT pc.Name AS row_group, psc.Name AS row_label, cr.Name AS col_group, st.Name AS col_label, SUM(sod.LineTotal) AS value FROM ...", "rowDimLabel":"Products", "colDimLabel":"Territories", "valueLabel":"Revenue ($)" }

doughnut.promptDescription: - **doughnut** — same as pie with a centre hole. Omit xLabel/yLabel. Spec: { "type":"doughnut", "title":"...", "labels":[...], "datasets":[{"label":"...","data":[...]}] }

dynet3d.promptDescription: - **dynet3d** — 3D dynamic-network visualization. Each time step renders as a parallel 2D network plane stacked along the z-axis; same-id nodes are connected across slices to form entity timelines. Drag to orbit, scroll to zoom; Reset to Front view collapses to 2D; Side view exposes the time axis. Best for showing how a network evolves over time. Spec (lazy SQL form): { "type":"dynet3d", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT day::text AS time, src_ip AS source, dst_ip AS target, attack_type AS category, COUNT(*) AS value FROM events GROUP BY day, src_ip, dst_ip, attack_type ORDER BY day" }. Spec (inline snapshots form): { "type":"dynet3d", "title":"...", "timeLabels":["Jan","Feb"], "nodes":[{"id":"u1","name":"Alice","type":"user"}], "snapshots":[{"time":"Jan","links":[{"source":"u1","target":"r1","value":5}]}] }. Spec (flat events form — preferred when data is a list of timestamped edges): { "type":"dynet3d", "title":"...", "events":[{"year":2023,"source":"Alice","target":"Bob","category":"collab","value":1}] }. The events form accepts year/time/period/date/month as the time key, source/src/from and target/dst/to as endpoints, category/type for link color, value/count for weight. Recognised columns: time (or period/date/day/month), source (or src/from), target (or dst/to), optional value/count, optional category/type (link color), optional source_type/target_type. When the time/date values are real timestamps or date strings (e.g. 2024-03-15, 2024-03-15T14:30, epoch seconds), the control panel shows a "Time unit" selector that re-buckets the stacked time-slices by year/month/day/hour/minute/second on the fly — to get the finest control, select the RAW timestamp as time (e.g. ts AS time) instead of pre-bucketing it in SQL. IMPORTANT — do not write custom Three.js rendering code. Always produce a spec object and pass it to the renderScript. If you must reference OrbitControls in any standalone Three.js snippet, use THREE.OrbitControls (not a bare OrbitControls global) — the Three.js r131 examples/js build attaches it to the THREE namespace.

nodetrix.promptDescription: - **nodetrix** — explore a network/graph with community structure: tightly-connected groups are drawn as adjacency-matrix blocks and the sparse links between groups as a node-link layout, avoiding the hairball a plain force diagram produces on dense data. Communities are detected automatically, so you only supply nodes + links. **Use lazy SQL** for non-trivial datasets — return one row per edge with `source`, `target`, `value` columns; the node list is derived automatically. Spec: (lazy SQL form) { "type":"nodetrix", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT s.ip_address AS source, d.ip_address AS target, COUNT(*) AS value FROM net90.traffic_90min t JOIN net90.ip_map s ON s.ip_id=t.src_ip JOIN net90.ip_map d ON d.ip_id=t.dst_ip GROUP BY 1,2" }. Spec: (inline form, small examples only) { "type":"nodetrix", "title":"...", "nodes":[{"name":"A"},{"name":"B"},{"name":"C"}], "links":[{"source":0,"target":1,"value":1}], "clusters":[[0,1,2]] }. `clusters` is optional (auto-detected when omitted); link source/target may be node indices or node names. Optional `maxMatrices` (number) keeps only the N largest communities as matrix blocks and renders the rest as individual nodes; optional `dropOrphans` (boolean) hides individual nodes that have no edge to any kept matrix community — together they tame fragmented hub-and-spoke graphs. Render-side note: a right-hand control panel lets the user switch the grouping algorithm (Louvain, Label Prop, Components, or provided clusters) and tune visual settings (cell size, max matrix size, max matrices, link opacity, labels, drop-orphans); clicking any matrix cell, matrix block, or single node shows its details, and blocks are draggable with scroll-to-zoom.

swimlane.promptDescription:
- **swimlane** — D3-powered time-axis diagram showing events arranged on horizontal lanes (one lane per actor/role/agent). X axis = time; Y axis = actor lane. Each event is a circular marker colored by category, with the full message available on hover. Optional curved arrows draw causal links between events when an event row carries a `cause_id` referring to another row's `event_id`. Use this when the user asks about a SEQUENCE of events, what LED TO an outcome, a TIMELINE of decisions across multiple actors, or a CHRONOLOGY with specific message content. Pairs well with sankey: sankey shows causal flow magnitude as boxes-and-flows; swimlane shows the same story positioned on a time axis with the actual message text surfaced on hover. IMPORTANT rules:
(1) Use lazy SQL — run the query first to verify it, then embed it as `"sql":"SELECT..."` in the spec; the browser fetches data via /query.
(2) Required column aliases (use these EXACT names): `time` (ISO timestamp, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS', 'HH:MM', or numeric), `actor` (the LANE identifier — typically agent_role, team, or department; NOT the timestamp), `label` (short event name shown on the marker — keep under ~30 chars).
(3) Optional columns: `message` (full message text shown on hover — keep under ~400 chars per row), `category` (groups events by color and adds a togglable legend), `value` (numeric — scales marker size), `event_id` and `cause_id` (both optional — when both present on related rows, a dashed arrow is drawn from cause to effect).
(4) Do NOT add a LIMIT clause unless the user explicitly asks. Order rows by time (ORDER BY time).
(5) Keep lane count under ~12 for readability — if many actors, filter to the ones involved in the event window.
(6) COMMON MISTAKE TO AVOID: do NOT use the timestamp as the `actor` column. The actor must be the role/agent name (e.g. 'legal', 'pr', 'social_media'). The timestamp goes in the `time` column only.
Spec: { "type":"swimlane", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT event_time AS time, agent_role AS actor, short_event AS label, content AS message, event_category AS category FROM communications WHERE ... ORDER BY event_time", "actors":["Legal","PR","Social"] }. The optional `actors` array fixes lane order; otherwise lanes appear in the order encountered in the data.

timearcs-gl.promptDescription: - **timearcs-gl** — WebGL-accelerated TimeArcs for large datasets. Same spec format and data semantics as `timearcs` (year/source/target/category/value, entity collapsing, force-ordered rows, year axis, activity bars, pan/zoom). Use this instead of `timearcs` whenever the result set is expected to exceed ~50k arcs (the plain SVG renderer drops frames around 10k–50k arcs and stalls into the hundreds of thousands; this renderer scales to millions). Identical SQL rules apply: lazy `"sql":"SELECT..."`, columns `year` (integer), `source`, `target`, `category` (STRONGLY RECOMMENDED — drives arc color coding and the per-category toggle legend; omitting it renders all arcs grey with no legend), optional `value`. For interactive time bucketing, also alias a raw timestamp/datetime column as `time` (col AS time, not pre-bucketed) to enable a TIME UNIT selector in the control panel (year/month/day/hour/minute/second); `year` remains the fallback when no `time` is provided. Always alias the category column as `category`. Spec: { "type":"timearcs-gl", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT EXTRACT(year FROM col)::int AS year, src AS source, dst AS target, type AS category, COUNT(*) AS value FROM tbl GROUP BY 1,2,3,4", "timeRange":[startInt, endInt] }. Interaction notes vs `timearcs`: no per-arc hover tooltip; arc category colors and the toggle legend (click a category swatch to show/hide its arcs) work fully; row-label click for group expand/collapse also works. If the user needs per-arc tooltip on a small dataset, prefer plain `timearcs`.

timearcs.promptDescription: - **timearcs** — temporal arc diagram showing pairwise relationships between named entities over time. Each event draws a semicircular arc (curving right) at the event's time position, connecting two entity rows. Arc thickness = value/count; color = category. Best for co-occurrence, collaboration, attack flows, or interaction networks across time. IMPORTANT rules: (1) `year` must be a plain integer — for datetime columns use EXTRACT(year FROM col)::int or DATE_PART; for finer granularity use YYYYMM (e.g. 202403) or day-of-year integer. (2) Use lazy SQL: run the query first to verify it, then embed that exact SQL as `"sql":"SELECT..."` in the spec — the browser fetches the full data via /query. Column aliases must be: year, source, target (or destination), category (optional), value (optional, default COUNT(*)). For INTERACTIVE time bucketing, also alias a raw timestamp/datetime column as `time` (e.g. col AS time) WITHOUT pre-bucketing it: when present, the control panel shows a TIME UNIT selector so the user can re-bucket arcs by year/month/day/hour/minute/second on the fly. Prefer row-level or finely-aggregated SQL (e.g. GROUP BY the raw timestamp, source, target, category) so the finer units have data; `year` is still used as a fallback when no `time` is given. (3) Aggregate with GROUP BY (year, source, target, category) + COUNT(*) AS value unless the user needs row-level detail. (4) Do NOT restrict entities or add a top-level LIMIT on the final result — return all matching rows so the visualization can display all entities. Spec: { "type":"timearcs", "title":"...", "sql":"SELECT EXTRACT(year FROM col)::int AS year, src AS source, dst AS target, type AS category, COUNT(*) AS value FROM tbl GROUP BY 1,2,3,4", "timeRange":[startInt, endInt] }. Omit timeRange to auto-detect. Render-side note: entities can be grouped using the control panel (Louvain, k-Core, Label Prop, IP Octets, or None). Large datasets start fully collapsed. Click ▶ to expand a group, click a red entity label to collapse its parent. Toolbar buttons expand/collapse all L1/L2 groups at once.

violin.promptDescription: - **violin** — violin plot; like a box plot but also shows the density shape of the distribution. Prefer over box when sample size is large enough to reveal distribution shape. Spec: { "type":"violin", "title":"...", "yLabel":"...", "data":[{"label":"Category A","values":[10,20,30,25,15]}] }

waterfall.promptDescription: - **waterfall** — shows the cumulative effect of sequential incremental values (e.g. month-over-month revenue change, cost breakdown). Use "measure":"total" for the final bar. Spec: { "type":"waterfall", "title":"...", "xLabel":"...", "yLabel":"...", "labels":["Opening","Jan","Feb","Closing"], "values":[1000000,200000,-80000,1120000], "measure":["absolute","relative","relative","total"] }

wordstream.promptDescription: - **wordstream** — animated word-stream visualization showing how word frequencies evolve across time periods, organized by topic/category. Each category occupies a flowing band where word font sizes reflect frequency; hover highlights matching words across all streams; click a word to extract its individual stream with an animated transition, click the stream background to restore. Best for exploring vocabulary evolution, keyword trends across topics, or how language in a text corpus shifts over time. Use lazy SQL: write a query that returns columns period, category, word, frequency, then embed it as "sql":"SELECT..." — the browser fetches and pivots the rows automatically. Column aliases must be: period (time bucket as text), category (topic/group), word (the term), frequency (COUNT or weight). CRITICAL SQL rules: (1) regexp_split_to_table and unnest are set-returning functions — they MUST appear only in the SELECT list or a LATERAL join, NEVER in WHERE. (2) window functions like row_number() CANNOT appear in WHERE — wrap them in a CTE and filter in the next step. Always use this 3-CTE pattern: WITH words AS (SELECT date_trunc('hour', ts)::text AS period, role AS category, regexp_split_to_table(lower(content), '\W+') AS word FROM tbl WHERE content IS NOT NULL AND content <> ''), counted AS (SELECT period, category, word, COUNT(*) AS frequency FROM words WHERE char_length(word) >= 3 AND word NOT SIMILAR TO '%[^a-z]%' AND word NOT IN ('the','and','that','this','with','from','have','will','are','for','not','but','can','its','was','has') GROUP BY period, category, word), ranked AS (SELECT *, row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY period, category ORDER BY frequency DESC) AS rn FROM counted) SELECT period, category, word, frequency FROM ranked WHERE rn <= 40 ORDER BY period, category, frequency DESC. Spec: { "type":"wordstream", "title":"...", "sql":"<query above>", "topWord":40, "minFont":10, "maxFont":25 }. Notes: SQL must produce at least 2 distinct period values. Omit sql and pass data[] directly as [{"date":"Label", "words":{"Category":[{"text":"word","frequency":15}]}}] when data is already in memory.
